title: Hierarchical Clustering: Building a Family Tree of Data
concept: Hierarchical clustering repeatedly merges the closest groups of points until everything belongs to one tree-shaped structure, so you never have to pick the number of clusters in advance.

exercises.prompt: Recap: K-Means was used for tasks like customer segmentation and image color compression — but what does K-Means require you to decide before it even starts?

options.text: The exact coordinates of every data point
Which cluster each point belongs to, in advance

exercises.explain: Right! K-Means needs you to pick K upfront. Today's algorithm, hierarchical clustering, skips that requirement entirely.

cards.title: Building a family tree from photos
cards.body: Imagine a huge pile of family photos with no labels. You'd start by pairing the two people who look most alike, then pair that small group with the next-most-similar group, and keep going until every photo connects into one giant family tree. That's exactly how hierarchical clustering finds structure in data — no one tells it how many branches the tree should have.

cards.title: What is hierarchical clustering?
cards.body: Hierarchical clustering is an algorithm that repeatedly merges the two closest groups of data points into a single, larger group — over and over — until every point has merged into one big cluster. The result is a tree-like structure called a dendrogram that records every merge along the way.

cards.title: A zipper, not a cookie cutter
cards.body: K-Means is like a cookie cutter — you decide the shape and number of cookies before you press down. Hierarchical clustering is more like slowly zipping fabric together: it keeps stitching the closest two pieces together one merge at a time, and you can stop the zipper wherever you like to see however many groups you want.

cards.title: Merging blobs of paint
cards.body: Picture dabs of paint scattered on a table. The two closest dabs blend first, forming a bigger blob. That blob then blends with whichever dab or blob is nearest to it. Keep going, and eventually every dab has merged into one giant blob — exactly how hierarchical clustering merges data points step by step.

cards.title: You don't pick K upfront
cards.body: You might think you need to decide the number of clusters before running hierarchical clustering, just like K-Means. In reality, the algorithm builds the entire tree first, showing every possible grouping from 1 cluster all the way to N clusters — you choose how many clusters you want by cutting the tree afterward, not before.

cards.title: Dendrogram vs. Flat Clusters
cards.body: How the tree turns into groups you can use:

left.text:
Shows every merge step
from individual points
to one giant cluster

right.text:
Slice at any height
to get however many
clusters you want

exercises.prompt: What is the tree-shaped output of hierarchical clustering called?
exercises.explain: Correct! A dendrogram shows every merge, from individual points up to one big cluster.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In hierarchical clustering, you must specify the number of clusters (K) before the algorithm runs.
exercises.explain: False — hierarchical clustering builds the full tree of merges first; you decide how many clusters to keep afterward by cutting the tree at a chosen height.

exercises.sentence: Hierarchical clustering merges the ___ two groups together at each step until only one cluster remains.
exercises.explain: At every step, hierarchical clustering merges whichever two groups are currently most similar (closest), building the tree from the ground up.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes a dendrogram?

options.text: A branching tree diagram where each merge shows two groups joining based on similarity
A bar chart showing the size of each cluster
A scatterplot of two features with no grouping shown

exercises.explain: Exactly — a dendrogram is the branching tree that records every merge hierarchical clustering makes.

exercises.prompt: A biologist has samples from 50 unidentified plant species and has no idea how many distinct species groupings actually exist in the data. Which approach lets her explore all possible numbers of groups without deciding in advance?

options.text: Hierarchical clustering, because it builds a full tree of merges she can cut at any level to test different numbers of groups
K-Means with K=50, because more clusters always means more accuracy
Hierarchical clustering, because it requires knowing the species count first
No clustering algorithm can be used without knowing the number of groups first

exercises.explain: Exactly! Hierarchical clustering builds the entire dendrogram regardless of K, so she can inspect the tree and decide how many species groupings make sense afterward. Next up: the two directions you can build that tree from.
